title: Gigot d'Agneau
subtitle: French roast leg of lamb with garlic, rosemary, and a proper pan jus
description: Gigot d'agneau is the French roast leg of lamb: a bone-in leg studded with slivers of garlic and rosemary, rubbed with olive oil and mustard, then roasted at a high initial temperature and finished at moderate heat. Unlike the British approach which tends toward very long, very low cooking, the French version aims for pink in the centre — internal temperature around 60–63 °C — and a shorter roasting time. The resulting meat is juicy and slightly rosy, served with a simple pan jus made from the deglazed drippings.
sourceNotes: Gigot d'agneau is a centrepiece of the French Easter table and a standard of French Sunday roasting. Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) details the garlic-studded preparation; Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) contains a closely related recipe with the pan jus.

recipeTools.notes: Large enough to hold the leg of lamb with some room around it.
Essential — target 60 °C for pink (medium-rare), 63 °C for slightly pink (medium), 70 °C for well done. The French preference is pink.
For making the incisions to stud the lamb with garlic.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin, unthickened sauce made by deglazing a roasting tin with liquid. The fond from the drippings dissolves into the wine or stock, producing a light, intensely flavoured sauce.
Leaving cooked meat off the heat before carving. Juices redistribute through the muscle fibres rather than running out onto the board.

ingredients.notes: About 2.2 kg — ask the butcher to leave the bone in. Remove from the fridge 1 hour before cooking.
Cut each into 3–4 slivers for studding
Cut into small pieces for studding, plus 2 whole sprigs to roast alongside

steps: Remove the lamb from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Heat the oven to 220 °C fan (240 °C / gas 9).
Using a paring knife, make small incisions all over the lamb — about 30 incisions, 2 cm deep, spread evenly across the leg. Push a sliver of garlic and a small piece of rosemary into each incision.
Mix the Dijon mustard and olive oil and spread it all over the surface of the lamb. Season generously with flaky salt and black pepper.
Place the lamb on a rack in the roasting tin, fat-side up. Tuck the rosemary sprigs underneath. Roast at 220 °C fan for 15 minutes.

Reduce the oven to 190 °C fan (210 °C / gas 7) and roast for a further 55–70 minutes — timing depends on the joint size and your preferred pinkness. Use a thermometer: 60 °C for rosy pink throughout, 63 °C for slightly pink, 70 °C for well done.
Transfer the lamb to a warm board, tent loosely with foil, and rest for 20 minutes.

Make the jus: place the roasting tin over a medium hob heat. Pour in the white wine and bring to a vigorous bubble, scraping up all the fond. Add the hot stock and simmer for 3–4 minutes until lightly reduced. Strain into a warm jug. Taste and season.
Carve the lamb across the grain and serve with the jus alongside.

content.text: The garlic-and-rosemary studding is what makes this dish French rather than simply a roast leg of lamb. Take the time to make incisions at regular intervals all over the joint — the garlic sweetens as the lamb roasts and perfumes the meat from the inside. A thermometer is essential; the French aim for a rosy pink interior and the window between 60 °C (rosy) and 70 °C (grey and dry) is narrow.

Heat the oven to 220 °C fan. Make about 30 incisions all over {{leg-of-lamb-bone-in}} of lamb and push a garlic sliver and a small piece of rosemary into each. Spread the mustard and olive oil all over and season generously with flaky salt and pepper. Roast at 220 °C fan for 15 minutes, then reduce to 190 °C fan and roast for 55–70 minutes more, checking with a thermometer: 60 °C for pink, 63 °C for medium, 70 °C for well done.
